name: Tajuña Greenway and Uclés Trail Loop
name_original: Morata y Perales de Tajuña, Carabaña, Valdaracete – ruta circular
description: This difficult 60.2-mile road cycling route near Morata de Tajuña offers diverse scenery and 3053 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Road cycling around Morata De Tajuña is characterized by its location in the Tajuña river valley, offering a blend of natural beauty and historical interest. The region features fertile plains with extensive agricultural lands, including olive groves and vineyards, alongside gentle hills. A significant draw for cyclists is the Tajuña Greenway, a dedicated cycling track that follows the old Arganda train line, providing traffic-free sections. The area is also part of the Southeast Regional Park, encompassing diverse ecosystems along the river courses. Best road cycling routes around Morata De Tajuña The most popular road cycling route is Tajuña Greenway and Uclés Trail Loop, a 60.2 miles (96.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 43 minutes to complete. This route incorporates sections of the Tajuña Greenway, traversing varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Tajuña River and Chinchón Loop, a difficult 74.6 miles (120.0 km) path. This route follows the Tajuña River, passing through the region's agricultural areas and offering views of the river valley. Local road cyclists also love the Tajuña Greenway loop from Morata de Tajuña, a 19.8 miles (31.9 km) trail leading through the Tajuña river valley, often completed in about 1 hour 39 minutes. Road cycling around Morata De Tajuña is defined by the Tajuña river valley, extensive olive groves, and the dedicated Tajuña Greenway. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Morata De Tajuña are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 3,600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Morata De Tajuña's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Morata de Tajuña?
A: Morata de Tajuña offers a wide variety of road cycling routes, with over 130 different options available. These routes cater to all skill levels, including 13 easy, 82 moderate, and 35 difficult trails.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Morata de Tajuña?
A: The terrain around Morata de Tajuña is diverse, characterized by the Tajuña river valley with its fertile plains, extensive olive groves, and vineyards. You'll also encounter gentle hills and sections of the dedicated Tajuña Greenway, which offers a smooth, traffic-free asphalt surface. Some routes may include varied surfaces, so it's good to be aware of potential terrain changes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes traverse the picturesque Tajuña river valley, offering views of agricultural lands. You might also encounter natural features within the Southeast Regional Park, such as the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2620629" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campillo Lagoon&lt;/a&gt;, a valuable reserve for waterfowl, or the distinct gorge landscape of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5123913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jarama Cliffs&lt;/a&gt;. The Tajuña Greenway itself passes by old railway stations and industrial vestiges like lime kilns.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by bike?
A: Yes, the region has historical interest. You can explore sites related to the Spanish Civil War, such as remnants near Cerro Vallequillas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33885652" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Morata de Tajuña Town Square – Tajuña Greenway loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a chance to experience the town's heritage. While not directly on all cycling routes, the Museo de la Molinería (Mill Museum) showcases the region's historical reliance on the Tajuña river's water power and is a pleasant stop.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Morata de Tajuña?
A: Absolutely. The Tajuña Greenway is particularly well-suited for beginners and offers superb conditions with its dedicated cycling track asphalt. An excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39561277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña Greenway loop from Morata de Tajuña&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 19.8-mile (31.9 km) trail that leads through the river valley.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes in Morata de Tajuña?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Morata de Tajuña offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977468183/tajuna-river-and-chinchon-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña River and Chinchón Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 74.6-mile (120.0 km) path with significant elevation gain, following the Tajuña River and passing through agricultural areas. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977088444/tajuna-greenway-and-ucles-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña Greenway and Uclés Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt;, covering 60.2 miles (96.9 km) with varied landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Morata de Tajuña?
A: The Tajuña river valley generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during those months. Winters are generally mild, making cycling possible, though you should check local weather conditions.

Q: Are the road cycling routes in Morata de Tajuña family-friendly?
A: Many sections, especially along the Tajuña Greenway, are very family-friendly. The Greenway is closed to traffic, has a smooth asphalt surface, and includes rest areas, making it suitable for cyclists of various ages and abilities. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39561277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña Greenway loop from Morata de Tajuña&lt;/a&gt; are excellent choices for families.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the road cycling trails, especially the Tajuña Greenway?
A: While the Tajuña Greenway is a popular route, specific regulations regarding dogs on cycling trails can vary. Generally, dogs on a leash are often permitted on greenways, but it's always advisable to check local signage or municipal guidelines for the most current rules to ensure a pleasant experience for everyone.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, there are several excellent circular road cycling routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39561277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña Greenway loop from Morata de Tajuña&lt;/a&gt; is a popular easy circular option. For longer, more challenging loops, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977468183/tajuna-river-and-chinchon-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña River and Chinchón Loop&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977088444/tajuna-greenway-and-ucles-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajuña Greenway and Uclés Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I park my car when cycling in Morata de Tajuña?
A: Morata de Tajuña, as a starting point for many routes, typically offers public parking options within the town. Look for designated parking areas near the town center or close to access points for the Tajuña Greenway. It's always a good idea to arrive early, especially on weekends, to secure a spot.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or accommodation for cyclists?
A: Morata de Tajuña and the towns along the Tajuña Greenway offer various amenities. You'll find cafes, restaurants, and local shops where you can refuel. For longer trips, there are accommodation options available in Morata de Tajuña and surrounding villages, catering to visitors exploring the region.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Morata de Tajuña?
A: The road cycling routes in Morata de Tajuña are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 3,600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the well-maintained Tajuña Greenway, the scenic river valley, and the diverse options for different skill levels.
tips: Fantastic way to cycle, without worrying about cars. Very far from the road, it allows you to enjoy the countryside. Very well paved, perfect for riding a road bike.
Nice bridge on the greenway of the Tajuña. It was originally built to cross over the old railway track which has now been converted into a fantastic cycle path.
It is a quiet and pretty section. The pine trees give a lot of shade, perfect when it is very hot in summer.

Mondéjar has the guarantee of the Designation of Origin of its wines (in 2016, the harvest was three million kilos of grapes). Cultural things that may interest you: The parish church of Santa María Magdalena, the hermitage of San Sebastián and the convent of San Antonio (although in ruins).
It is part of the old architectural complex of the city fortress and dates from the Middle Ages.
Nice stretch of tarmac, starts (from Morata) with a nice short climb and then continues with a series of rolling hills in between olive trees and spectacular views.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Morata y Perales de Tajuña, Carabaña, Valdaracete circular route for racebiking?
faqs.answer: This route is considered difficult for racebiking. While sections of the Tajuña Greenway are generally easy with gentle inclines, the full circular route extends beyond these flatter parts, incorporating significant climbs and descents. Expect varied gradients throughout the 97 km distance and 930 meters of elevation gain.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain will I encounter on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: The route features a mix of terrain. You'll cycle on reddish asphalt sections of the Vía Verde del Tajuña, which follows an old railway line. Beyond the greenway, expect rolling country roads, some shared with motorized traffic, and potentially compacted earth surfaces. The region is known for its 'endless rolling roads' and can include areas with 'red clay baked in'.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass several notable points of interest. These include the Tajuña Greenway itself, a Stone Arch Bridge on the Tajuña Greenway, and a Pine Forest Trail. You can also see the Parish Church of Santa María Magdalena of Mondéjar and the Castle of Villarejo de Salvanés. The Morata de Tajuña to Valdelaguna Scenic Road offers picturesque views.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse and picturesque scenery. You'll follow the Tajuña River, winding through meadows, scrubland, and rainfed crops. Expect extensive olive groves, cornfields, and other agricultural landscapes. The route passes through quiet villages and provides 'spectacular landscapes of Las Vegas de Madrid'. You'll also see historical elements like old railway stations and lime kilns, as well as natural features such as groves and remnants of old gypsum mines.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region south of Madrid, where this route is located, is often described as a 'cycling paradise'. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be chilly, so plan accordingly for weather conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It largely follows public roads and greenways.

faqs.question: What are the regulations for cycling within the Parque Regional del Sureste?
faqs.answer: This route is located within the Parque Regional del Sureste. While cycling is generally permitted on designated paths and roads, it's important to respect the natural environment. Stay on marked trails, do not disturb wildlife, and carry out all your waste. There are no specific entrance fees for the park itself.

faqs.question: Is this racebike route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While sections of the Vía Verde del Tajuña are generally accessible, this is primarily a racebike route that includes public roads and potentially faster speeds. For the safety of your dog and other cyclists, it is generally not recommended to bring dogs on a racebike tour of this nature. If you do, they must be kept on a leash and under strict control at all times, especially on shared paths.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route, and how can I reach it by public transport?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for the entire circular route aren't detailed, you can easily access the starting point of the Tajuña Greenway, a significant part of this route, from Madrid. Cyclists can take Metro Line 9 to Arganda del Rey, which provides convenient public transport access to the greenway.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths or greenways?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates significant portions of the Vía Verde del Tajuña. It also connects with the Grand Tour CiclaMadrid in Carabaña, integrating it into a broader cycling network within the region. You will also find sections of the Camino Santiaguista de Uclés and the Vía Verde del Tren los 40 días.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several 'sleepy little villages' such as Morata de Tajuña, Perales de Tajuña, Carabaña, and Valdaracete. These villages typically offer cafes, restaurants, or small shops where you can refuel and find refreshments.

faqs.question: Is this a loop route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as indicated by its name, 'Morata y Perales de Tajuña, Carabaña, Valdaracete – circular route', this is a loop route, meaning it starts and ends in the same general area without requiring an out-and-back return.
